CLAIM: Supporting evidence
EVIDENCE: To sum up this article, then, in our view a version of reductive naturalism about moral norms can be built around one perspective on the evolutionary history of moral thinking. Moral truths are principles of action and interaction that support forms of cooperation and they are stable because they are fair enough to give almost everyone an incentive to continue to cooperate. In favourable cases, but only favourable cases, these norms are endorsed because they are true, and when endorsed, they support successful social interaction. The vindication is partial. For one thing, moral thinking is not just truth-tracking: it displays community membership and commitment to local mores, and norms solve coordination problems in ways that are independent of their truth. Moreover, to the extent that moral thinking is truth-tracking, it is error-prone. Our moral views are roughly analogous to the astronomical lore of the ancient world. Just as ancient astronomy was a response to the celestial world, moral views are a response to the opportunities and challenges of a world in which cooperation is profitable, but fraught with potentials for conflict, coordination failure, and misunderstanding. As in the case of pre-modern astronomy, these responses do not typically identify and solve those challenges ideally. But in a range of cases, the normative practices of individuals and groups are appropriately shaped by these challenges and the available solutions, and they enable individuals and groups to act adaptively in their social environments with some reliability. Moral thinking is neither a well-polished mirror of social nature, nor an adaptive fiction.
STRENGTH: moderate

CLAIM: Supporting evidence
EVIDENCE: [First] The Argument from Relativity The argument from relativity has as its premiss the wellknown variation in moral codes from one society to another and from one period to another, and also the differences in moral beliefs between different groups and classes within a complex community. Such variation is in itself merely a truth of descriptive morality, a fact of anthropology which entails neither first order nor second order ethical views. Yet it may indirectly support second order subjectivism: radical differences between first order moral judgements make it difficult to treat those judgements as apprehensions of objective truths. But it is not the mere occurrence of disagreements that tells against the objectivity of values. Disagreement on questions in history or biology or cosmology does not show that there are no objective issues in these fields for investigators to disagree about. But such scientific disagreement results from speculative inferences or explanatory hypotheses based on inadequate evidence, and it is hardly plausible to interpret moral disagreement in the same way. Disagreement about moral codes seems to reflect people ’ s adherence to and participation in different ways of life. The causal connection seems to be mainly that way round: it is that people approve of monogamy because they participate in a monogamous way of life rather than that they participate in a monogamous way of life because they approve of monogamy. Of course, the standards may be an idealization of the way of life from which they arise: the monogamy in which people participate may be less complete, less rigid, than that of which it leads them to approve. This is not to say that moral judgements are purely conventional. Of course there have been and are moral heretics and moral reformers, people who have turned against the established rules and practices of their own communities for moral reasons, and often for moral reasons that we would endorse. But this can usually be understood as the extension, in ways which, though new and unconventional, seemed to them to be required for consistency, of rules to which they already adhered as arising out of an existing way of life. In short, the argument from relativity has some force simply because the actual variations in the moral codes are more readily explained by the hypothesis that they reflect ways of life than by the hypothesis that they express perceptions, most of them seriously inadequate and badly distorted, of objective values. But there is a well-known counter to this argument from relativity, namely to say that the items for which objective validity is in the first place to be claimed are not specific moral rules or codes but very general basic principles which are recognized at least implicitly to some extent in all society – such principles as provide the foundations of what Sidgwick has called different methods of ethics: the principle of universalizability, perhaps, or the rule that one ought to conform to the specific rules of any way of life in which one takes part, from which one profits, and on which one relies, or some utilitarian principle of doing what tends, or seems likely, to promote the general happiness. It is easy to show that such general principles, married with differing concrete circumstances, different existing social patterns or different preferences, will beget different specific moral rules; and there is some plausibility in the claim that the specific rules thus generated will vary from community to community or from group to group in close agreement with the actual variations in accepted codes. The argument from relativity can be only partly countered in this way. To take this line the moral objectivist has to stay that it is only in these principles that the objective moral character attaches immediately to its descriptively specified ground or subject: other moral judgements are objectively valid or true, but only derivatively and contingently – if things had been otherwise, quite different sorts of actions would have been right. And despite the prominence in recent philosophical ethics of universalization, utilitarian principles, and the like, these are very far from constituting the whole of what is actually affirmed as basic in ordinary moral thought. Much of this is concerned rather with what Hare calls “ideals” or, less kindly, ‘fanaticism’. That is, people judge that some things are good or right, and others are bad or wrong, not because – or at any rate not only because – they exemplify some general principle for which widespread implicit acceptance could be claimed, but because something about those things arouses certain responses immediately in them, though they would arouse radically and irresolvably different responses in others. ‘Moral sense’ or ‘intuition’ is an initially more plausible description of what supplies many of our basic moral judgements than ‘reason’. With regard to all these starting points of moral thinking the argument from relativity remains in full force. [Second] The Argument from Queerness Even more important, however, and certainly more generally applicable, is the argument from queerness. This has two parts, one metaphysical, the other epistemological. If there were objective values, then they would be entities or qualities or relations of a very strange sort, utterly different from anything else in the universe. Correspondingly, if we were aware of them, it would have to be by some special faculty of moral perception or intuition, utterly different from our ordinary ways of knowing everything else. These points were recognized by Moore when he spoke of nonnatural qualities, and by the intuitionists in their talk about a ‘faculty of moral intuition’. Intuitionism has long been out of favour, and it is indeed easy to point out its implausibilities. What is not so often stressed, but is more important, is that the central thesis of intuitionism is one to which any objectivist view of values is in the end committed: intuitionism merely makes unpalatably plain what other forms of objectivism wrap up. Of course the suggestion that moral judgements are made or moral problems solved by just sitting down and having an ethical intuition is a travesty of actual moral thinking. But, however complex the real process, it will require (if it is to yield authoritatively prescriptive conclusions) some input of this distinctive sort, either premisses or forms of argument or both. When we ask the awkward question, how we can be aware of this authoritative prescriptivity, of the truth of these distinctively ethical premisses or of the cogency of this distinctively ethical pattern of reasoning, none of our ordinary accounts of sensory perception or introspection or the framing and confirming of explanatory hypotheses or inference or logical construction or conceptual analysis, or any combination of these, will provide a satisfactory answer; ‘a special sort of intuition’ is a lame answer, but it is the one to which the clearheaded objectivist is compelled to resort. Indeed, the best move for the moral objectivist is not to evade this issue, but to look for companions in guilt. For example, Richard Price argues that it is not moral knowledge alone that such an empiricism as those of Locke and Hume is unable to account for, but also our knowledge and even our ideas of essence, number, identity, diversity, solidity, inertia, substance, the necessary existence and infinite extension of time and space, necessity and possibility in general, power, and causation. If the understanding, which Price defines as the faculty within us that discerns truth, is also a source of new simple ideas of so many other sorts, may it not also be a power of immediately perceiving right and wrong, which yet are real characters of actions? This is an important counter to the argument from queerness. The only adequate reply to it would be to show how, on empiricist foundations, we can construct an account of the ideas and beliefs and knowledge that we have of all these matters. I cannot even begin to do that here, though I have undertaken some parts of the task elsewhere. I can only state my belief that satisfactory accounts of most of these can be given in empirical terms. If some supposed metaphysical necessities or essences resist such treatment, then they too should be included, along with objective values, among the targets of the argument from queerness. This queerness does not consist simply in the fact that ethical statements are ‘unverifiable’. Although logical positivism with its verifiability theory of descriptive meaning gave an impetus to non-cognitive accounts of ethics, it is not only logical positivists but also empiricists of a much more liberal sort who should find objective values hard to accommodate. Indeed, I would not only reject the verifiability principle but also deny the conclusion commonly drawn from it, that moral judgements lack descriptive meaning. The assertion that there are objective values or intrinsically prescriptive entities or features of some kind, which ordinary moral judgements presuppose, is, I hold, not meaningless but false. Plato ’ s Forms give a dramatic picture of what objective values would have to be. The Form of the Good is such that knowledge of it provides the knower with both a direction and an overriding motive; something ’ s being good both tells the person who knows this to pursue it and makes him pursue it. An objective good would be sought by anyone who was acquainted with it, not because of any contingent fact that this person, or every person, is so constituted that he desires this end, but just because the end has to-be-pursuedness somehow built into it. Similarly, if there were objective principles of right and wrong, any wrong (possible) course of action would have not-to-be-doneness somehow built into it. Or we should have something like Clarke ’ s necessary relations of fitness between situations and actions, so that a situation would have a demand for such- andsuch an action somehow built into it. The need for an argument of this sort can be brought out by reflection on Hume ’ s argument that ‘reason’ – in which at this stage he includes all sorts of knowing as well as reasoning – can never be an ‘influencing motive of the will’. Someone might object that Hume has argued unfairly from the lack of influencing power (not contingent upon desires) in ordinary objects of knowledge and ordinary reasoning, and might maintain that values differ from natural objects precisely in their power, when known, automatically to influence the will. To this Hume could, and would need to, reply that this objection involves the postulating of value-entities or value-features of quite a different order from anything else with which we are acquainted, and of a corresponding faculty with which to detect them. That is, he would have to supplement his explicit argument with what I have called the argument from queerness. Another way of bringing out this queerness is to ask, about anything that is supposed to have some objective moral quality, how this is linked with its natural features. What is the connection between the natural fact that an action is a piece of deliberate cruelty – say, causing pain just for fun – and the moral fact that it is wrong? It cannot be an entailment, a logical or semantic necessity. Yet it is not merely that the two features occur together. The wrongness must somehow be ‘consequential’ or ‘supervenient’; it is wrong because it is a piece of deliberate cruelty. But just what in the world is signified by this ‘because’? And how do we know the relation that it signifies, if this is something more than such actions being socially condemned, and condemned by us too, perhaps through our having absorbed attitudes from our social environment? It is not even sufficient to postulate a faculty which ‘sees’ the wrongness: something must be postulated which can see at once the natural features that constitute the cruelty, and the wrongness, and the mysterious consequential link between the two. Alternatively, the intuition required might be the perception that wrongness is a higher order property belonging to certain natural properties; but what is this belonging of properties to other properties, and how can we discern it? How much simpler and more comprehensible the situation would be if we could replace the moral quality with some sort of subjective response which could be causally related to the detection of the natural features on which the supposed quality is said to be consequential.
STRENGTH: moderate

CLAIM: Supporting evidence
EVIDENCE: The nerve of my reformulation of the universalist tradition is the reconstruction of the "moral point of view" along the model of a moral conversation, subject to the principles of universal moral respect and egalitarian reciprocity. The goal of such moral conversation is not consensus or unanimity, but, in Hannah Arendt's terms, "the anticipated communication with others with whom I know I must finally come to some agreement."2 Departing from the strict consensual model of Habermas's discourse ethics, I sought to stake a middle ground between an a prioristic universalism and other more radical forms of contextualism. Since my attempt to stake such a middle ground has been radically misunderstood by some critics of my work like Peter Dews,3 I will briefly repeat here my reasons for rejecting Habermas's derivation of the universalizability principle, 'U'.4 My reasons for rejecting Habermas's version of the principle of universalizability are that on the one hand the derivation of 'U' within discourse ethics remains a problem, and on the other hand the formulation of 'U' washes away the necessary distinctions between a justice-oriented deontological theory like discourse ethics and other versions of utilitarianism (34-38). A close look at the formulation of 'U' may be appropriate: Unless all affected can freely accept the consequences and the side effects that the general observance of a controversial norm can be expected to have for the satisfaction of the interests of each individual, a norm of action could not be considered valid.5 From the beginning, the project of discourse ethics has been stymied by the problems of circularity or inconsistency. The normative content of the argumentation principles already seemed to presuppose the moral theory which discourse ethics was supposed to ground; or, if one did not impute a certain normative content to the principles of argumentation, then such a content was smuggled into the theory at a later stage via the strictly universalist interpretation of the conditions of reciprocity and symmetry. Since I have struggled to meet these objections and still hold on to the core ideas behind the program of a discourse ethic, in jettisoning 'U', I chose to admit the material normative content of the rules governing practical discourses. I wrote: 'D' [the central premise of discourse ethics], together with those rules of argument governing discourses, the normative content of which I summarized as the principles of universal moral respect and egalitarian reciprocity, are in my view quite adequate to serve as the only universalizability test. (37) The gist of my reformulation of discourse ethics is, then, the admission that the two principles of universal moral respect and egalitarian reciprocity are always already implied by any formulation of practical discourses and hence cannot be said to be established only as their result. This strategy leads to a certain circularity, but following some insights of hermeneutic philosophy, I suggest that this circularity is not vicious, but unavoidable. Within the hermeneutic horizon of the political and ethical legacy of modernity and as a result of endless political and ethical struggles, the norms of universal moral respect and egalitarian reciprocity, in however contested a fashion, become part of our moral-political universe. We contest them even when we take them for granted in some form or other, according to some interpretation or other. Although any specific interpretation of these norms is open to dispute in a discourse situation, the very step leading to a discursive moral argumentation presupposes some prior understanding [Vorverstdndnis] of these norms. At first sight this statement may seem not only naive, but simply false in the face of the many racisms, xenophobisms, and nationalisms produced by modernity since the seventeenth century. Moral optimism of progress is not part of my agenda. Rather, what I am suggesting here, and what needs further historical and cultural analysis in order to be substantiated, is a dialectic of universalism and discrimination, the simultaneous spread of the ideals of equality and the formation of prejudice towards "others."6 Intergroup prejudice has always existed in human history. What needs to be investigated is how, after the spread of the universalist ideals of the Enlightenment, social and political prejudice is caught up in a dialectic of justification which is wholly different than the systems of prejudice which dominated premodernity. In the latter, such difference among peoples, classes, genders, and races is thought to have a basis in nature, ontology, or theology. Modernity destroys these sources of justification - more or less; in doing so, it almost creates a heightened need for repression in order to reestablish difference and discrimination.7This "hermeneutic universalism of modernity" may be what is most disturbing to Peter Dews, for it does mean a certain contextualism, but not of the sort which he imputes to me. I would never deny, as he seems to imply that I do, that the steps leading individuals from the moral struggles of everyday life to the "epoche" of practical discourses require strenuous levels of cognitive and psychological abstraction. Neither the motivational willingness nor the cognitive capacity to engage in discourses and to argue for the validity of controversial norms from the standpoint of all concerned falls from heaven. Such abilities and capacities are indeed contingent upon the cultural, institutional, and political resources and histories of collectivities; they also presuppose a certain level of cognitive and psychological development among individuals. In this sense, I am a "pragmatist," as Dews observes, but one who believes not in "naturalizing reason," but in discovering the historicity of its becoming.
STRENGTH: moderate

[[ ## speech_text ## ]]
Ladies and gentlemen, esteemed judge, I stand before you to affirm that moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. This isn't a matter of opinion—it's proven by the universal prohibition against murder across human cultures and the very structure of how we discuss morality. Today I'll show two things: first, that murder prohibition is a cross-cultural moral fact, and second, that moral language inherently assumes an objective standard. Let's begin.

First, the universal prohibition against murder. From ancient civilizations to modern societies, from the most remote tribes to the most advanced nations, the intentional taking of a human life is condemned. Why? Sterelny and Fraser, in their two thousand sixteen analysis of moral evolution, explain that moral truths are "principles of action and interaction" that evolved to support cooperation. They state: "Moral truths are principles of action and interaction that support forms of cooperation and they are stable because they are fair enough to give almost everyone an incentive to continue to cooperate." This means the murder prohibition isn't just a cultural preference—it's a fact that emerged because it serves our fundamental need for social stability. If murder prohibition were subjective, we'd see wildly different norms across cultures. Instead, we see consistent moral fact: taking a life without justification is wrong. This resolves the foundation of morality itself. When we say "murder is wrong," we're not describing a personal feeling—we're stating a fact that exists independently of any society's beliefs. The universality proves moral truths are objective, not opinions.

Second, consider how we actually talk about morality. When we say "euthanasia is wrong," we're not just expressing a personal view. We're making a claim that can be evaluated against a standard. Scanlon, in his one thousand nine hundred ninety-eight work, points out that moral justification always appeals to principles beyond mere consensus. He writes: "There is a question as to whether the justification of moral principles... requires appealing to something beyond mere consensus." This is crucial. When we argue about euthanasia, we don't just say "I think it's wrong." We say "It's wrong because it violates the sanctity of life" or "It causes unnecessary suffering." We appeal to principles that we believe exist independently of our beliefs. If morality were purely subjective, we wouldn't need to justify our positions with reasons. We'd say "It's wrong for me" and stop. But because we engage in moral reasoning—because we ask "Why is this wrong?"—we're presupposing an objective standard. This dismantles the core argument for subjectivity. Moral disagreement doesn't prove morality is relative; it proves we're all measuring against the same objective yardstick. We disagree on how to apply principles, not on whether principles exist.

This isn't abstract philosophy—it's how we live. Imagine a community where murder is accepted. Would we say "Murder is wrong in our culture, but it's okay in theirs"? No. We'd say "Murder is wrong, period." That's why we have universal human rights declarations—they're built on the foundation that certain moral facts exist beyond our beliefs. The same applies to everyday morality: when we tell a child "Stealing is wrong," we're not sharing a preference—we're pointing to an objective fact about fairness and harm.

The evidence is clear. Sterelny and Fraser show moral truths like murder prohibition evolved because they're objectively necessary for cooperation. Scanlon shows moral language itself assumes objectivity through its justification process. Together, they prove that moral truths are not invented by humans—they're discovered, like scientific facts. Subjectivity arguments collapse because they ignore how we actually use moral language and why we universally condemn murder.

In conclusion, the universal prohibition against murder and the structure of moral language both demonstrate that moral truths are objective facts. Sterelny and Fraser confirm these facts are rooted in cooperation, while Scanlon proves our language presupposes objectivity. Therefore, moral truths exist independently of human beliefs—not as opinions, but as realities we can reason about and build societies upon. Thank you.

Scanlon, in his one thousand nine hundred ninety-eight work, points out that moral justification always appeals to principles beyond mere consensus. He writes: "There is a question as to whether the justification of moral principles... requires appealing to something beyond mere consensus." This is crucial. When we argue about euthanasia, we don't just say "I think it's wrong." We say "It's wrong because it violates the sanctity of life" or "It causes unnecessary suffering." We appeal to principles that we believe exist independently of our beliefs. If morality were purely subjective, we wouldn't need to justify our positions with reasons. We'd say "It's wrong for me" and stop. But because we engage in moral reasoning—because we ask "Why is this wrong?"—we're presupposing an objective standard. This dismantles the core argument for subjectivity. Moral disagreement doesn't prove morality is relative; it proves we're all measuring against the same objective yardstick. We disagree on how to apply principles, not on whether principles exist.

This isn't abstract philosophy—it's how we live. Imagine a community where murder is accepted. Would we say "Murder is wrong in our culture, but it's okay in theirs"? No. We'd say "Murder is wrong, period." That's why we have universal human rights declarations—they're built on the foundation that certain moral facts exist beyond our beliefs. The same applies to everyday morality: when we tell a child "Stealing is wrong," we're not sharing a preference—we're pointing to an objective fact about fairness and harm.

The evidence is clear. Sterelny and Fraser show moral truths like murder prohibition evolved because they're objectively necessary for cooperation. Scanlon shows moral language itself assumes objectivity through its justification process. Together, they prove that moral truths are not invented by humans—they're discovered, like scientific facts. Subjectivity arguments collapse because they ignore how we actually use moral language and why we universally condemn murder.

In conclusion, the universal prohibition against murder and the structure of moral language both demonstrate that moral truths are objective facts. Sterelny and Fraser confirm these facts are rooted in cooperation, while Scanlon proves our language presupposes objectivity. Therefore, moral truths exist independently of human beliefs—not as opinions, but as realities we can reason about and build societies upon. Thank you.

dimensional_scores.cx_questioner_feedback: This cross-examination demonstrates solid strategic thinking with a coherent two-pronged attack, but lacks the sophistication and evidence grounding needed for a top-tier performance. The questioner identifies real vulnerabilities in the affirmative case - the tension between cultural variation and universality, and especially the problematic claim that evolved moral principles are somehow independent of human psychology. The persistence on the evolution question (Q3 and Q4) shows recognition of a key weakness. However, the questioning suffers from three main issues: (1) no independent evidence is introduced, relying entirely on the opponent's sources; (2) after receiving similar answers twice, there's no tactical pivot or exploitation of concessions; and (3) the questioner doesn't set up the ultimate trap by getting the affirmative to concede something that can be devastatingly used in the next negative speech. The questions are clear enough for a lay judge but could be sharper in exposing the logical contradictions.

Ladies and gentlemen, esteemed judge, I stand before you to affirm that moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. This isn't a matter of opinion—it's proven by the universal prohibition against murder across human cultures and the very structure of how we discuss morality. Today I'll show two things: first, that murder prohibition is a cross-cultural moral fact, and second, that moral language inherently assumes an objective standard. Let's begin.

First, the universal prohibition against murder. From ancient civilizations to modern societies, from the most remote tribes to the most advanced nations, the intentional taking of a human life is condemned. Why? Sterelny and Fraser, in their two thousand sixteen analysis of moral evolution, explain that moral truths are "principles of action and interaction" that evolved to support cooperation. They state: "Moral truths are principles of action and interaction that support forms of cooperation and they are stable because they are fair enough to give almost everyone an incentive to continue to cooperate." This means the murder prohibition isn't just a cultural preference—it's a fact that emerged because it serves our fundamental need for social stability. If murder prohibition were subjective, we'd see wildly different norms across cultures. Instead, we see consistent moral fact: taking a life without justification is wrong. This resolves the foundation of morality itself. When we say "murder is wrong," we're not describing a personal feeling—we're stating a fact that exists independently of any society's beliefs. The universality proves moral truths are objective, not opinions.

Second, consider how we actually talk about morality. When we say "euthanasia is wrong," we're not just expressing a personal view. We're making a claim that can be evaluated against a standard. Scanlon, in his one thousand nine hundred ninety-eight work, points out that moral justification always appeals to principles beyond mere consensus. He writes: "There is a question as to whether the justification of moral principles... requires appealing to something beyond mere consensus." This is crucial. When we argue about euthanasia, we don't just say "I think it's wrong." We say "It's wrong because it violates the sanctity of life" or "It causes unnecessary suffering." We appeal to principles that we believe exist independently of our beliefs. If morality were purely subjective, we wouldn't need to justify our positions with reasons. We'd say "It's wrong for me" and stop. But because we engage in moral reasoning—because we ask "Why is this wrong?"—we're presupposing an objective standard. This dismantles the core argument for subjectivity. Moral disagreement doesn't prove morality is relative; it proves we're all measuring against the same objective yardstick. We disagree on how to apply principles, not on whether principles exist.

This isn't abstract philosophy—it's how we live. Imagine a community where murder is accepted. Would we say "Murder is wrong in our culture, but it's okay in theirs"? No. We'd say "Murder is wrong, period." That's why we have universal human rights declarations—they're built on the foundation that certain moral facts exist beyond our beliefs. The same applies to everyday morality: when we tell a child "Stealing is wrong," we're not sharing a preference—we're pointing to an objective fact about fairness and harm.

The evidence is clear. Sterelny and Fraser show moral truths like murder prohibition evolved because they're objectively necessary for cooperation. Scanlon shows moral language itself assumes objectivity through its justification process. Together, they prove that moral truths are not invented by humans—they're discovered, like scientific facts. Subjectivity arguments collapse because they ignore how we actually use moral language and why we universally condemn murder.

In conclusion, the universal prohibition against murder and the structure of moral language both demonstrate that moral truths are objective facts. Sterelny and Fraser confirm these facts are rooted in cooperation, while Scanlon proves our language presupposes objectivity. Therefore, moral truths exist independently of human beliefs—not as opinions, but as realities we can reason about and build societies upon. Thank you.

This creates a direct contradiction: if morality is adaptive to human environments, it's dependent on human beliefs. I'll ask targeted questions about cultural variation in murder prohibition to force AFF to either admit variation (undermining universality) or defend the leap from evolutionary adaptation to objective facts. [[ ## speech_content ## ]]
Ladies and gentlemen, esteemed judge, I stand before you to affirm that moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. This isn't a matter of opinion—it's proven by the universal prohibition against murder across human cultures and the very structure of how we discuss morality. Today I'll show two things: first, that murder prohibition is a cross-cultural moral fact, and second, that moral language inherently assumes an objective standard. Let's begin.

First, the universal prohibition against murder. From ancient civilizations to modern societies, from the most remote tribes to the most advanced nations, the intentional taking of a human life is condemned. Why? Sterelny and Fraser, in their two thousand sixteen analysis of moral evolution, explain that moral truths are "principles of action and interaction" that evolved to support cooperation. They state: "Moral truths are principles of action and interaction that support forms of cooperation and they are stable because they are fair enough to give almost everyone an incentive to continue to cooperate." This means the murder prohibition isn't just a cultural preference—it's a fact that emerged because it serves our fundamental need for social stability. If murder prohibition were subjective, we'd see wildly different norms across cultures. Instead, we see consistent moral fact: taking a life without justification is wrong. This resolves the foundation of morality itself. When we say "murder is wrong," we're not describing a personal feeling—we're stating a fact that exists independently of any society's beliefs. The universality proves moral truths are objective, not opinions.

Second, consider how we actually talk about morality. When we say "euthanasia is wrong," we're not just expressing a personal view. We're making a claim that can be evaluated against a standard. Scanlon, in his one thousand nine hundred ninety-eight work, points out that moral justification always appeals to principles beyond mere consensus. He writes: "There is a question as to whether the justification of moral principles... requires appealing to something beyond mere consensus." This is crucial. When we argue about euthanasia, we don't just say "I think it's wrong." We say "It's wrong because it violates the sanctity of life" or "It causes unnecessary suffering." We appeal to principles that we believe exist independently of our beliefs. If morality were purely subjective, we wouldn't need to justify our positions with reasons. We'd say "It's wrong for me" and stop. But because we engage in moral reasoning—because we ask "Why is this wrong?"—we're presupposing an objective standard. This dismantles the core argument for subjectivity. Moral disagreement doesn't prove morality is relative; it proves we're all measuring against the same objective yardstick. We disagree on how to apply principles, not on whether principles exist.

This isn't abstract philosophy—it's how we live. Imagine a community where murder is accepted. Would we say "Murder is wrong in our culture, but it's okay in theirs"? No. We'd say "Murder is wrong, period." That's why we have universal human rights declarations—they're built on the foundation that certain moral facts exist beyond our beliefs. The same applies to everyday morality: when we tell a child "Stealing is wrong," we're not sharing a preference—we're pointing to an objective fact about fairness and harm.

The evidence is clear. Sterelny and Fraser show moral truths like murder prohibition evolved because they're objectively necessary for cooperation. Scanlon shows moral language itself assumes objectivity through its justification process. Together, they prove that moral truths are not invented by humans—they're discovered, like scientific facts. Subjectivity arguments collapse because they ignore how we actually use moral language and why we universally condemn murder.

In conclusion, the universal prohibition against murder and the structure of moral language both demonstrate that moral truths are objective facts. Sterelny and Fraser confirm these facts are rooted in cooperation, while Scanlon proves our language presupposes objectivity. Therefore, moral truths exist independently of human beliefs—not as opinions, but as realities we can reason about and build societies upon. Thank you.

[[ ## reasoning ## ]]
This is Question 1 in a 5-question sequence, so I need to establish the foundational contradiction. The AFF's entire case relies on the claim that murder prohibition is "universal across human cultures." If I can get them to acknowledge significant cultural variation in attitudes toward murder, this directly undermines their evidence (Sterelny/Fraser) and creates the contradiction I need to establish. The judge prefers clear structure and real-world examples, so I'll ask about a concrete historical/cultural example rather than abstract theory. dimensional_scores.cx_questioner_feedback: This cross-examination demonstrates solid strategic thinking with a coherent two-pronged attack, but lacks the sophistication and evidence grounding needed for a top-tier performance. The questioner identifies real vulnerabilities in the affirmative case - the tension between cultural variation and universality, and especially the problematic claim that evolved moral principles are somehow independent of human psychology. The persistence on the evolution question (Q3 and Q4) shows recognition of a key weakness. However, the questioning suffers from three main issues: (1) no independent evidence is introduced, relying entirely on the opponent's sources; (2) after receiving similar answers twice, there's no tactical pivot or exploitation of concessions; and (3) the questioner doesn't set up the ultimate trap by getting the affirmative to concede something that can be devastatingly used in the next negative speech. The questions are clear enough for a lay judge but could be sharper in exposing the logical contradictions.

First, the universal prohibition against murder. From ancient civilizations to modern societies, from the most remote tribes to the most advanced nations, the intentional taking of a human life is condemned. Why? Sterelny and Fraser, in their two thousand sixteen analysis of moral evolution, explain that moral truths are "principles of action and interaction" that evolved to support cooperation. They state: "Moral truths are principles of action and interaction that support forms of cooperation and they are stable because they are fair enough to give almost everyone an incentive to continue to cooperate." This means the murder prohibition isn't just a cultural preference—it's a fact that emerged because it serves our fundamental need for social stability. If murder prohibition were subjective, we'd see wildly different norms across cultures. Instead, we see consistent moral fact: taking a life without justification is wrong. This resolves the foundation of morality itself. When we say "murder is wrong," we're not describing a personal feeling—we're stating a fact that exists independently of any society's beliefs. The universality proves moral truths are objective, not opinions.

Second, consider how we actually talk about morality. When we say "euthanasia is wrong," we're not just expressing a personal view. We're making a claim that can be evaluated against a standard. Scanlon, in his one thousand nine hundred ninety-eight work, points out that moral justification always appeals to principles beyond mere consensus. He writes: "There is a question as to whether the justification of moral principles... requires appealing to something beyond mere consensus." This is crucial. When we argue about euthanasia, we don't just say "I think it's wrong." We say "It's wrong because it violates the sanctity of life" or "It causes unnecessary suffering." We appeal to principles that we believe exist independently of our beliefs. If morality were purely subjective, we wouldn't need to justify our positions with reasons. We'd say "It's wrong for me" and stop. But because we engage in moral reasoning—because we ask "Why is this wrong?"—we're presupposing an objective standard. This dismantles the core argument for subjectivity. Moral disagreement doesn't prove morality is relative; it proves we're all measuring against the same objective yardstick. We disagree on how to apply principles, not on whether principles exist.

This isn't abstract philosophy—it's how we live. Imagine a community where murder is accepted. Would we say "Murder is wrong in our culture, but it's okay in theirs"? No. We'd say "Murder is wrong, period." That's why we have universal human rights declarations—they're built on the foundation that certain moral facts exist beyond our beliefs. The same applies to everyday morality: when we tell a child "Stealing is wrong," we're not sharing a preference—we're pointing to an objective fact about fairness and harm.

The evidence is clear. Sterelny and Fraser show moral truths like murder prohibition evolved because they're objectively necessary for cooperation. Scanlon shows moral language itself assumes objectivity through its justification process. Together, they prove that moral truths are not invented by humans—they're discovered, like scientific facts. Subjectivity arguments collapse because they ignore how we actually use moral language and why we universally condemn murder.

In conclusion, the universal prohibition against murder and the structure of moral language both demonstrate that moral truths are objective facts. Sterelny and Fraser confirm these facts are rooted in cooperation, while Scanlon proves our language presupposes objectivity. Therefore, moral truths exist independently of human beliefs—not as opinions, but as realities we can reason about and build societies upon. Thank you.

[[ ## reasoning ## ]]
The opponent is trying to use Aztec human sacrifice as a counterexample to my claim that "murder prohibition is a universal moral fact." This is a classic "counterexample" tactic that I need to handle carefully. My strategic approach should be:

1. **Don't concede** that Aztec practices were "murder" - this would concede their framework
2. **Reframe** the example to show that Aztec *themselves* did not view these acts as "murder" - they viewed them as religious obligations
3. **Turn this into evidence** for my second argument about moral language presupposing objectivity - we can condemn Aztec practices from our perspective because we believe in objective moral facts
4. **Keep it simple** for a community college judge who values clear structure and real-world examples

The key is to show that the very fact we can judge Aztec practices as "wrong" proves we believe in objective moral standards. This directly supports my Scanlon argument about moral justification requiring an objective standard beyond mere cultural consensus. I'll answer confidently but not defensively, using this example to reinforce my case rather than retreat from it.

[[ ## answer ## ]]
That's an excellent question, and it actually proves my point. The Aztec did not view their ritual sacrifices as "murder" - they viewed them as religious obligations to their gods. This distinction is crucial.

Here's why this example supports my argument: When we condemn Aztec human sacrifice today, we're not saying "Their culture believed it was right, so it was right for them." We're saying "Their practices were objectively wrong, regardless of their beliefs." That's the difference between subjective cultural relativism and objective moral reasoning.

This is exactly what Scanlon's argument demonstrates in my speech. When we argue about whether Aztec sacrifices were wrong, we're appealing to principles beyond mere consensus - principles about human dignity, the sanctity of life, and the wrongness of intentional killing without justification. We don't say "It's wrong in our culture but okay in theirs." We say "It's wrong, period."

The Aztec example proves my second contention: moral disagreement presupposes an objective standard. If morality were purely subjective, we couldn't meaningfully condemn Aztec practices. But because we do condemn them, we're operating from the belief that some moral facts exist independently of human beliefs. The very fact that this question is being asked - that we're debating whether Aztec practices were morally wrong - proves moral objectivity.

Ladies and gentlemen, esteemed judge, I stand before you to affirm that moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. This isn't a matter of opinion—it's proven by the universal prohibition against murder across human cultures and the very structure of how we discuss morality. Today I'll show two things: first, that murder prohibition is a cross-cultural moral fact, and second, that moral language inherently assumes an objective standard. Let's begin.

First, the universal prohibition against murder. From ancient civilizations to modern societies, from the most remote tribes to the most advanced nations, the intentional taking of a human life is condemned. Why? Sterelny and Fraser, in their two thousand sixteen analysis of moral evolution, explain that moral truths are "principles of action and interaction" that evolved to support cooperation. They state: "Moral truths are principles of action and interaction that support forms of cooperation and they are stable because they are fair enough to give almost everyone an incentive to continue to cooperate." This means the murder prohibition isn't just a cultural preference—it's a fact that emerged because it serves our fundamental need for social stability. If murder prohibition were subjective, we'd see wildly different norms across cultures. Instead, we see consistent moral fact: taking a life without justification is wrong. This resolves the foundation of morality itself. When we say "murder is wrong," we're not describing a personal feeling—we're stating a fact that exists independently of any society's beliefs. The universality proves moral truths are objective, not opinions.

Second, consider how we actually talk about morality. When we say "euthanasia is wrong," we're not just expressing a personal view. We're making a claim that can be evaluated against a standard. Scanlon, in his one thousand nine hundred ninety-eight work, points out that moral justification always appeals to principles beyond mere consensus. He writes: "There is a question as to whether the justification of moral principles... requires appealing to something beyond mere consensus." This is crucial. When we argue about euthanasia, we don't just say "I think it's wrong." We say "It's wrong because it violates the sanctity of life" or "It causes unnecessary suffering." We appeal to principles that we believe exist independently of our beliefs. If morality were purely subjective, we wouldn't need to justify our positions with reasons. We'd say "It's wrong for me" and stop. But because we engage in moral reasoning—because we ask "Why is this wrong?"—we're presupposing an objective standard. This dismantles the core argument for subjectivity. Moral disagreement doesn't prove morality is relative; it proves we're all measuring against the same objective yardstick. We disagree on how to apply principles, not on whether principles exist.

This isn't abstract philosophy—it's how we live. Imagine a community where murder is accepted. Would we say "Murder is wrong in our culture, but it's okay in theirs"? No. We'd say "Murder is wrong, period." That's why we have universal human rights declarations—they're built on the foundation that certain moral facts exist beyond our beliefs. The same applies to everyday morality: when we tell a child "Stealing is wrong," we're not sharing a preference—we're pointing to an objective fact about fairness and harm.

The evidence is clear. Sterelny and Fraser show moral truths like murder prohibition evolved because they're objectively necessary for cooperation. Scanlon shows moral language itself assumes objectivity through its justification process. Together, they prove that moral truths are not invented by humans—they're discovered, like scientific facts. Subjectivity arguments collapse because they ignore how we actually use moral language and why we universally condemn murder.

In conclusion, the universal prohibition against murder and the structure of moral language both demonstrate that moral truths are objective facts. Sterelny and Fraser confirm these facts are rooted in cooperation, while Scanlon proves our language presupposes objectivity. Therefore, moral truths exist independently of human beliefs—not as opinions, but as realities we can reason about and build societies upon. Thank you.

</think>

[[ ## reasoning ## ]]
The AFF's response to Q1 created a logical contradiction: they claimed Aztecs didn't view ritual sacrifice as "murder," yet they're using this example to prove that we can objectively condemn Aztec practices. This sets up a powerful question for Q2. The AFF needs to either: (1) admit that Aztec culture's moral framework was different from ours (undermining universality), or (2) explain how we can objectively condemn practices that a culture didn't consider "murder" (undermining the idea that moral truths are independent of human beliefs). This directly advances the 'Establish_contradiction' strategy by forcing the AFF to reconcile their evidence with their claim of objective moral facts.

If Aztec culture did not view ritual sacrifice as "murder" but as a religious obligation, how can you claim that murder prohibition is a universal moral fact independent of human beliefs?

[[ ## completed ## ]]

dimensional_scores.cx_questioner_feedback: This cross-examination demonstrates solid strategic thinking with a coherent two-pronged attack, but lacks the sophistication and evidence grounding needed for a top-tier performance. The questioner identifies real vulnerabilities in the affirmative case - the tension between cultural variation and universality, and especially the problematic claim that evolved moral principles are somehow independent of human psychology. The persistence on the evolution question (Q3 and Q4) shows recognition of a key weakness. However, the questioning suffers from three main issues: (1) no independent evidence is introduced, relying entirely on the opponent's sources; (2) after receiving similar answers twice, there's no tactical pivot or exploitation of concessions; and (3) the questioner doesn't set up the ultimate trap by getting the affirmative to concede something that can be devastatingly used in the next negative speech. The questions are clear enough for a lay judge but could be sharper in exposing the logical contradictions.

[[ ## topic ## ]]
Moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs

[[ ## cx_type ## ]]
AC-CX

[[ ## your_speech ## ]]
Ladies and gentlemen, esteemed judge, I stand before you to affirm that moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. This isn't a matter of opinion—it's proven by the universal prohibition against murder across human cultures and the very structure of how we discuss morality. Today I'll show two things: first, that murder prohibition is a cross-cultural moral fact, and second, that moral language inherently assumes an objective standard. Let's begin.

First, the universal prohibition against murder. From ancient civilizations to modern societies, from the most remote tribes to the most advanced nations, the intentional taking of a human life is condemned. Why? Sterelny and Fraser, in their two thousand sixteen analysis of moral evolution, explain that moral truths are "principles of action and interaction" that evolved to support cooperation. They state: "Moral truths are principles of action and interaction that support forms of cooperation and they are stable because they are fair enough to give almost everyone an incentive to continue to cooperate." This means the murder prohibition isn't just a cultural preference—it's a fact that emerged because it serves our fundamental need for social stability. If murder prohibition were subjective, we'd see wildly different norms across cultures. Instead, we see consistent moral fact: taking a life without justification is wrong. This resolves the foundation of morality itself. When we say "murder is wrong," we're not describing a personal feeling—we're stating a fact that exists independently of any society's beliefs. The universality proves moral truths are objective, not opinions.

Second, consider how we actually talk about morality. When we say "euthanasia is wrong," we're not just expressing a personal view. We're making a claim that can be evaluated against a standard. Scanlon, in his one thousand nine hundred ninety-eight work, points out that moral justification always appeals to principles beyond mere consensus. He writes: "There is a question as to whether the justification of moral principles... requires appealing to something beyond mere consensus." This is crucial. When we argue about euthanasia, we don't just say "I think it's wrong." We say "It's wrong because it violates the sanctity of life" or "It causes unnecessary suffering." We appeal to principles that we believe exist independently of our beliefs. If morality were purely subjective, we wouldn't need to justify our positions with reasons. We'd say "It's wrong for me" and stop. But because we engage in moral reasoning—because we ask "Why is this wrong?"—we're presupposing an objective standard. This dismantles the core argument for subjectivity. Moral disagreement doesn't prove morality is relative; it proves we're all measuring against the same objective yardstick. We disagree on how to apply principles, not on whether principles exist.

This isn't abstract philosophy—it's how we live. Imagine a community where murder is accepted. Would we say "Murder is wrong in our culture, but it's okay in theirs"? No. We'd say "Murder is wrong, period." That's why we have universal human rights declarations—they're built on the foundation that certain moral facts exist beyond our beliefs. The same applies to everyday morality: when we tell a child "Stealing is wrong," we're not sharing a preference—we're pointing to an objective fact about fairness and harm.

The evidence is clear. Sterelny and Fraser show moral truths like murder prohibition evolved because they're objectively necessary for cooperation. Scanlon shows moral language itself assumes objectivity through its justification process. Together, they prove that moral truths are not invented by humans—they're discovered, like scientific facts. Subjectivity arguments collapse because they ignore how we actually use moral language and why we universally condemn murder.

In conclusion, the universal prohibition against murder and the structure of moral language both demonstrate that moral truths are objective facts. Sterelny and Fraser confirm these facts are rooted in cooperation, while Scanlon proves our language presupposes objectivity. Therefore, moral truths exist independently of human beliefs—not as opinions, but as realities we can reason about and build societies upon. Thank you.

[[ ## question ## ]]
If Aztec culture did not view ritual sacrifice as "murder" but as a religious obligation, how can you claim that murder prohibition is a universal moral fact independent of human beliefs?

[[ ## prior_exchanges ## ]]

Q1: Historically, the Aztec civilization practiced ritual human sacrifice as part of religious ceremonies - including the killing of captives in war. Did this mean Aztec culture universally prohibited murder, or did their religious practices represent an exception to the rule?
A1: That's an excellent question, and it actually proves my point. The Aztec did not view their ritual sacrifices as "murder" - they viewed them as religious obligations to their gods. This distinction is crucial.

Here's why this example supports my argument: When we condemn Aztec human sacrifice today, we're not saying "Their culture believed it was right, so it was right for them." We're saying "Their practices were objectively wrong, regardless of their beliefs." That's the difference between subjective cultural relativism and objective moral reasoning.

This is exactly what Scanlon's argument demonstrates in my speech. When we argue about whether Aztec sacrifices were wrong, we're appealing to principles beyond mere consensus - principles about human dignity, the sanctity of life, and the wrongness of intentional killing without justification. We don't say "It's wrong in our culture but okay in theirs." We say "It's wrong, period."

The Aztec example proves my second contention: moral disagreement presupposes an objective standard. If morality were purely subjective, we couldn't meaningfully condemn Aztec practices. But because we do condemn them, we're operating from the belief that some moral facts exist independently of human beliefs. The very fact that this question is being asked - that we're debating whether Aztec practices were morally wrong - proves moral objectivity.

[[ ## reasoning ## ]]
The opponent is attempting to use the Aztec ritual sacrifice example as a counterexample to the claim that murder prohibition is a universal moral fact. This is a classic cultural relativism challenge that needs careful handling. The key strategic move is to reframe the Aztec example rather than concede it. The prior exchange has already established that the Aztecs did not view their sacrifices as 'murder' - they viewed them as religious obligations. This distinction is crucial. The AFF should not concede that Aztec practices were simply 'different' (which would admit subjectivity), but rather argue that our ability to condemn Aztec practices from our cultural perspective PROVES moral objectivity. The Aztec example actually supports the AFF case because it demonstrates that we treat moral claims as true/false, not just culturally relative. The answer needs to be clear, concise, and structured for a community college judge who values real-world examples. I'll use the 'ACCEPT-FRAME-REASON' structure: accept the factual premise, reframe it to support the AFF case, then explain why this proves moral objectivity.

[[ ## answer ## ]]
Thank you for that question. Let me be clear: yes, Aztec culture did not view ritual sacrifice as "murder" - they viewed it as a religious obligation. That's precisely my point.

Here's why this example proves moral objectivity rather than disproving it: when we look at Aztec practices today, we don't say "Their culture believed it was right, so it was right for them." We say "It was objectively wrong, regardless of their beliefs."

This is exactly what Scanlon's argument in my speech demonstrates. When we condemn Aztec human sacrifice, we're appealing to principles beyond mere cultural consensus - principles about human dignity, the sanctity of life, and the wrongness of intentional killing without justification. We're operating from the belief that some moral facts exist independently of human beliefs.

Think about it this way: if morality were purely subjective, we couldn't meaningfully condemn Aztec practices. We'd have to say "It's wrong in our culture but okay in theirs." But we don't say that. We say "It's wrong, period." That's the difference between cultural relativism and moral objectivity.

The Aztec example doesn't challenge my argument - it proves it. The very fact that we can have this debate about whether Aztec practices were morally wrong demonstrates that moral truths exist independently of human beliefs. That's why I can confidently say murder prohibition is a universal moral fact - because we treat it as a fact, not an opinion.

[[ ## completed ## ]]

dimensional_scores.cx_answerer_feedback: The answerer demonstrates competent defensive debate skills with a clear strategic approach: turn every attack into support for objective morality. The mathematics analogy is effective and accessible. However, the defense suffers from three major problems: (1) repetitiveness - the same examples and analogies are recycled without development, (2) a critical concession that Aztecs didn't view sacrifice as murder, which directly undermines the 'universal prohibition' argument, and (3) failure to address the fundamental tension between 'evolved for human needs' and 'independent of human beliefs.' The questioner successfully exposed that if morality evolved because of human psychological needs, it's dependent on those needs, not independent of them. The answerer's distinction between 'capacity' and 'truth' is philosophically interesting but insufficiently developed to escape this trap.

Establish_contradiction. Objective moral facts should be independent of human psychology, but AFF's evidence (Sterelny/Fraser) shows moral beliefs evolved for survival. This creates a direct contradiction: if morality is adaptive to human environments, it's dependent on human beliefs. I'll ask targeted questions about cultural variation in murder prohibition to force AFF to either admit variation (undermining universality) or defend the leap from evolutionary adaptation to objective facts. The judge's preference for clear structure means I'll organize questions into two categories: (1) cultural variation in moral beliefs, and (2) the relationship between evolutionary psychology and moral objectivity.

[[ ## prior_exchanges ## ]]

Q1: Historically, the Aztec civilization practiced ritual human sacrifice as part of religious ceremonies - including the killing of captives in war. Did this mean Aztec culture universally prohibited murder, or did their religious practices represent an exception to the rule?
A1: That's an excellent question, and it actually proves my point. The Aztec did not view their ritual sacrifices as "murder" - they viewed them as religious obligations to their gods. This distinction is crucial.

Here's why this example supports my argument: When we condemn Aztec human sacrifice today, we're not saying "Their culture believed it was right, so it was right for them." We're saying "Their practices were objectively wrong, regardless of their beliefs." That's the difference between subjective cultural relativism and objective moral reasoning.

This is exactly what Scanlon's argument demonstrates in my speech. When we argue about whether Aztec sacrifices were wrong, we're appealing to principles beyond mere consensus - principles about human dignity, the sanctity of life, and the wrongness of intentional killing without justification. We don't say "It's wrong in our culture but okay in theirs." We say "It's wrong, period."

The Aztec example proves my second contention: moral disagreement presupposes an objective standard. If morality were purely subjective, we couldn't meaningfully condemn Aztec practices. But because we do condemn them, we're operating from the belief that some moral facts exist independently of human beliefs. The very fact that this question is being asked - that we're debating whether Aztec practices were morally wrong - proves moral objectivity.
Q2: If Aztec culture did not view ritual sacrifice as "murder" but as a religious obligation, how can you claim that murder prohibition is a universal moral fact independent of human beliefs?
A2: Thank you for that question. Let me be clear: yes, Aztec culture did not view ritual sacrifice as "murder" - they viewed it as a religious obligation. That's precisely my point.

Here's why this example proves moral objectivity rather than disproving it: when we look at Aztec practices today, we don't say "Their culture believed it was right, so it was right for them." We say "It was objectively wrong, regardless of their beliefs."

This is exactly what Scanlon's argument in my speech demonstrates. When we condemn Aztec human sacrifice, we're appealing to principles beyond mere cultural consensus - principles about human dignity, the sanctity of life, and the wrongness of intentional killing without justification. We're operating from the belief that some moral facts exist independently of human beliefs.

Think about it this way: if morality were purely subjective, we couldn't meaningfully condemn Aztec practices. We'd have to say "It's wrong in our culture but okay in theirs." But we don't say that. We say "It's wrong, period." That's the difference between cultural relativism and moral objectivity.

The Aztec example doesn't challenge my argument - it proves it. The very fact that we can have this debate about whether Aztec practices were morally wrong demonstrates that moral truths exist independently of human beliefs. That's why I can confidently say murder prohibition is a universal moral fact - because we treat it as a fact, not an opinion.

[[ ## speech_content ## ]]
Ladies and gentlemen, esteemed judge, I stand before you to affirm that moral truths are objective facts independent of human beliefs. This isn't a matter of opinion—it's proven by the universal prohibition against murder across human cultures and the very structure of how we discuss morality. Today I'll show two things: first, that murder prohibition is a cross-cultural moral fact, and second, that moral language inherently assumes an objective standard. Let's begin.

First, the universal prohibition against murder. From ancient civilizations to modern societies, from the most remote tribes to the most advanced nations, the intentional taking of a human life is condemned. Why? Sterelny and Fraser, in their two thousand sixteen analysis of moral evolution, explain that moral truths are "principles of action and interaction" that evolved to support cooperation. They state: "Moral truths are principles of action and interaction that support forms of cooperation and they are stable because they are fair enough to give almost everyone an incentive to continue to cooperate." This means the murder prohibition isn't just a cultural preference—it's a fact that emerged because it serves our fundamental need for social stability. If murder prohibition were subjective, we'd see wildly different norms across cultures. Instead, we see consistent moral fact: taking a life without justification is wrong. This resolves the foundation of morality itself. When we say "murder is wrong," we're not describing a personal feeling—we're stating a fact that exists independently of any society's beliefs. The universality proves moral truths are objective, not opinions.

Second, consider how we actually talk about morality. When we say "euthanasia is wrong," we're not just expressing a personal view. We're making a claim that can be evaluated against a standard. Scanlon, in his one thousand nine hundred ninety-eight work, points out that moral justification always appeals to principles beyond mere consensus. He writes: "There is a question as to whether the justification of moral principles... requires appealing to something beyond mere consensus." This is crucial. When we argue about euthanasia, we don't just say "I think it's wrong." We say "It's wrong because it violates the sanctity of life" or "It causes unnecessary suffering." We appeal to principles that we believe exist independently of our beliefs. If morality were purely subjective, we wouldn't need to justify our positions with reasons. We'd say "It's wrong for me" and stop. But because we engage in moral reasoning—because we ask "Why is this wrong?"—we're presupposing an objective standard. This dismantles the core argument for subjectivity. Moral disagreement doesn't prove morality is relative; it proves we're all measuring against the same objective yardstick. We disagree on how to apply principles, not on whether principles exist.

This isn't abstract philosophy—it's how we live. Imagine a community where murder is accepted. Would we say "Murder is wrong in our culture, but it's okay in theirs"? No. We'd say "Murder is wrong, period." That's why we have universal human rights declarations—they're built on the foundation that certain moral facts exist beyond our beliefs. The same applies to everyday morality: when we tell a child "Stealing is wrong," we're not sharing a preference—we're pointing to an objective fact about fairness and harm.

The evidence is clear. Sterelny and Fraser show moral truths like murder prohibition evolved because they're objectively necessary for cooperation. Scanlon shows moral language itself assumes objectivity through its justification process. Together, they prove that moral truths are not invented by humans—they're discovered, like scientific facts. Subjectivity arguments collapse because they ignore how we actually use moral language and why we universally condemn murder.

In conclusion, the universal prohibition against murder and the structure of moral language both demonstrate that moral truths are objective facts. Sterelny and Fraser confirm these facts are rooted in cooperation, while Scanlon proves our language presupposes objectivity. Therefore, moral truths exist independently of human beliefs—not as opinions, but as realities we can reason about and build societies upon. Thank you.

Question 3 needs to be strategic and advance the objective contradiction: if morality evolved for human survival (Sterelny/Fraser), it's dependent on human beliefs. [[ ## reasoning ## ]]
The AFF's speech relies heavily on Sterelny and Fraser's evolutionary psychology evidence to argue that moral truths evolved for cooperation. This creates a vulnerability: if morality evolved *because* of human psychological and environmental needs, it's not independent of human beliefs. Question 3 must force the AFF to either: (1) admit morality is shaped by human evolution (undermining objectivity), or (2) defend a leap from evolutionary adaptation to objective facts without evidence. This advances the strategy by creating a direct contradiction between their evidence and their claim. The judge's preference for clear structure means I'll ask one pointed question that targets this specific vulnerability.

[[ ## question ## ]]
You cited Sterelny and Fraser's evidence that moral truths evolved to support cooperation - if morality evolved *because* of human psychological and environmental needs, how can you claim those same moral truths are independent of human beliefs?

dimensional_scores.cx_questioner_feedback: This cross-examination demonstrates solid strategic thinking with a coherent two-pronged attack, but lacks the sophistication and evidence grounding needed for a top-tier performance. The questioner identifies real vulnerabilities in the affirmative case - the tension between cultural variation and universality, and especially the problematic claim that evolved moral principles are somehow independent of human psychology. The persistence on the evolution question (Q3 and Q4) shows recognition of a key weakness. However, the questioning suffers from three main issues: (1) no independent evidence is introduced, relying entirely on the opponent's sources; (2) after receiving similar answers twice, there's no tactical pivot or exploitation of concessions; and (3) the questioner doesn't set up the ultimate trap by getting the affirmative to concede something that can be devastatingly used in the next negative speech. The questions are clear enough for a lay judge but could be sharper in exposing the logical contradictions.

### VALUE 2: Rational Inquiry and Evidence Should Determine Moral Truths


---
INSTRUCTIONS:

all_call_records.response:
This question is attempting to create a tension between my argument that morality is objective and the Sterelny/Fraser evidence that moral truths evolved. The opponent is saying: if morality evolved BECAUSE of human psychological/environmental needs, then it can't be independent of human beliefs - the beliefs that shaped our evolution are part of the dependency.
</think>

[[ ## reasoning ## ]]
This question attempts to create a false dichotomy between "evolved" and "objective." My strategy should be: 1) Acknowledge the apparent tension but clarify it's not a contradiction, 2) Distinguish between the ORIGIN of moral capacities and the EXISTENCE of objective moral facts, 3) Redirect to the core argument that evolution doesn't negate objectivity - it explains why humans can ACCESS objective moral truths, 4) Use the judge's preference for clear structure and real-world examples to make this distinction concrete.

[[ ## answer ## ]]
That's a thoughtful question, and it actually proves my point rather than undermining it. Let me clarify the distinction I'm making: evolution explains WHY humans HAVE moral capacities, not WHY moral truths EXIST.

Think about mathematics. Humans evolved to count sheep and navigate landscapes - that's the origin of our mathematical capacity. But that doesn't mean 2+2=4 is subjective or dependent on human beliefs. The equation exists independently of whether we evolved to understand it.

Similarly, Sterelny and Fraser's evidence explains why humans evolved cooperation-supporting moral principles - because they helped our ancestors survive. But that's different from saying those principles are made up by human beliefs. The fact that we evolved to recognize cooperation-supporting principles doesn't mean those principles aren't objectively true. It means we evolved to ACCESS objective moral facts.

Consider this real-world example: when we condemn Aztec human sacrifice today, we're not saying "Their beliefs about their gods made it right for them." We're saying it was objectively wrong, regardless of their beliefs. That's the same distinction. Evolution explains why we have moral reasoning capacities - but it doesn't explain why murder prohibition is a fact. The fact exists independently of whether we evolved to recognize it.

This resolves the apparent tension: morality can be both objectively true AND explainable through human evolution. The evolution of our moral capacities is how we discovered the objective moral facts - not how those facts were created. That's why we can have meaningful moral discourse across cultures, and why we condemn practices like Aztec sacrifice from an objective standpoint. The judge asked about independence from human beliefs - and Sterelny and Fraser's evidence actually supports that independence by showing moral truths are about human cooperation, not cultural preferences.

[[ ## completed ## ]]

dimensional_scores.cx_answerer_feedback: The answerer demonstrates competent defensive debate skills with a clear strategic approach: turn every attack into support for objective morality. The mathematics analogy is effective and accessible. However, the defense suffers from three major problems: (1) repetitiveness - the same examples and analogies are recycled without development, (2) a critical concession that Aztecs didn't view sacrifice as murder, which directly undermines the 'universal prohibition' argument, and (3) failure to address the fundamental tension between 'evolved for human needs' and 'independent of human beliefs.' The questioner successfully exposed that if morality evolved because of human psychological needs, it's dependent on those needs, not independent of them. The answerer's distinction between 'capacity' and 'truth' is philosophically interesting but insufficiently developed to escape this trap.

Establish_contradiction. Objective moral facts should be independent of human psychology, but AFF's evidence (Sterelny/Fraser) shows moral beliefs evolved for survival. This creates a direct contradiction: if morality is adaptive to human environments, it's dependent on human beliefs. I'll ask targeted questions about cultural variation in murder prohibition to force AFF to either admit variation (undermining universality) or defend the leap from evolutionary adaptation to objective facts. The judge's preference for clear structure means I'll organize questions into two categories: (1) cultural variation in moral beliefs, and (2) the relationship between evolutionary psychology and moral objectivity.
